data.title: Taking Stock of Vaxcyte (PCVX): Valuation After Encouraging VAX-31 Phase 2 Results and Bullish Analyst Outlook
data.description:
Recent analyst commentary around Vaxcyte (PCVX) zeroed in on encouraging Phase 2 data for its VAX-31 pneumococcal vaccine, sharpening investor focus on how the program could reshape the company’s prospects as it heads into pivotal Phase 3 trials.

See our latest analysis for Vaxcyte.

Those upbeat VAX-31 data have arrived after a choppy stretch, with the share price at $47.65 and a 7 day share price return of 10.66 percent. This contrasts with a year to date share price return of negative 42.80 percent and a 5 year total shareholder return of 64.54 percent, suggesting long term believers have still been rewarded even as near term volatility reflects shifting views on risk and execution.

If Vaxcyte’s story has you rethinking your exposure to healthcare innovators, this could be a good moment to explore other promising healthcare stocks that the market may be mispricing.

Price to Book of 2.2x: Is it justified?

Vaxcyte changes hands at a price to book ratio of 2.2 times, which screens as inexpensive relative to peers despite its lack of profits.

Price to book compares a company’s market value with the net assets on its balance sheet, a common yardstick for early stage biotechs that do not yet generate earnings or meaningful revenue. For Vaxcyte, this metric anchors investor expectations in tangible capital invested today rather than uncertain near term profitability, reflecting how the market weighs its vaccine pipeline potential against ongoing cash burn.

Compared with the average price to book of 13.2 times for similarly sized peers and 2.7 times for the broader US biotech industry, Vaxcyte’s 2.2 times multiple looks notably restrained. data.title: Did Yuexiu’s HK$500 Million, Shareholder‑Linked Loan Just Shift Yuexiu Property's (SEHK:123) Investment Narrative?
data.description:

  • An interesting feature of the deal is that a change in Yue Xiu Enterprises’ ownership or control below set thresholds could trigger an event of default, allowing the lender to demand immediate repayment.

To own Yuexiu Property today, you really have to believe the group can work through weak margins, heavy use of debt and softer revenue while still preserving enough financial flexibility to support its pipeline and dividend ambitions. The latest HK$500,000,000 term loan does not radically change that picture, but it reinforces how reliant Yuexiu is on short to medium term bank funding and on the continued support of its controlling shareholder, Yue Xiu Enterprises. Those ownership-linked default clauses effectively hard-wire governance stability into the credit story, but they also underline concentration risk if anything were to affect the parent. data.title: Biohaven (BHVN) Valuation Check After BHV-1510 Cancer Data and BHV-7000 Pipeline Shift
data.description:
Biohaven (BHVN) is back in the spotlight after unveiling promising BHV-1510 cancer data at ESMO and simultaneously pivoting away from BHV-7000 in depression to refocus on immunology, obesity, and epilepsy.

See our latest analysis for Biohaven.

Those BHV-1510 results and the strategic pivot away from depression look to be reshaping sentiment, with an 11.67% 1 month share price return offering a bright spot against a steep year to date share price decline and heavy 1 year total shareholder loss. This suggests momentum may be stabilizing rather than accelerating for now.

If this kind of clinical inflection has you rethinking your watchlist, it could be a good moment to scout other potential winners across healthcare stocks and see what else stands out.

Price-to-Book of -83.6x, Is it justified?

Biohaven’s headline valuation metric looks extreme at first glance, with a deeply negative price to book ratio reflecting a balance sheet under clear strain.

Price to book compares a company’s market value to its net assets, and it is a common shorthand for how much investors are paying over the accounting equity base.

In Biohaven’s case, liabilities currently exceed assets, leaving shareholders with negative equity and producing a mechanically negative price to book figure of -83.6x that is hard to interpret in the usual way.

That stands in stark contrast to both the broader US Biotechs industry, where the average price to book ratio is around 2.7x, and Biohaven’s peer group, which sits near 16.5x. This underscores just how unconventional the company’s capital structure has become.
